verdict: PASS — citation-density fix confirmed. Sections 5/6/7 now carry approximately 21 cited factual assertions out of ~27 standalone factual claims (~78%), well above the 60% floor. All other checks continue to pass: required sections present and non-trivial, no sequence-drift violations, word count within standard profile range (~4,100w, 3,000–4,400), no scope creep, beginner readability clean. One residual observation (non-blocking): three low-weight factual claims in sections 5.4 and 7.2 remain uncited ('Admissions offices process thousands of applications in a short window'; the two framing sentences opening the 7.2 mechanism explanation). These are contextual observations rather than research findings and do not affect the density calculation. Automation complacency in section 7.2 is correctly mention-and-deferred: bare name, no definition, explicit later-topic pointer — compliant.

Automation bias (9.5), Judgment Framework Q1/Q2/Q3 (9.7/9.8/9.9), acceptable error (9.10), high-stakes domains (9.11), and biases in training data (9.6 cross-ref) are all from prior topics in the sequence. length_sanity.detail: Corpus is approximately 4,100 words (sections 2–11 inclusive). Within the standard profile range of 3,000–4,400 words. No 30%-outside-range trigger.

topic_scope.topic_title: Case study: AI in college admissions — where was the human override point missed?
topic_scope.detail: All content in sections 5–9 is directly relevant to the headline case study question. Section 5.7 introduces 'training data' — which is in this topic's own concepts_introduced list — scoped narrowly to explaining why the missing override point matters in the admissions context; it does not teach the broader ML training pipeline. Section 8 governance controls and the audit methodology in section 6 are tightly bound to the admissions case evidence. Automation complacency in 7.2 is mention-and-defer (one sentence, bare name, no definition). No out-of-scope advanced concepts taught at depth.
